intro.overview: Before you can analyze network traffic, you need to understand network addresses. This lab builds your fluency with binary, hexadecimal, and CIDR notation — the foundational math that makes packet capture meaningful. You’ll calculate subnets by hand using a visual “count out” method, then verify your work with industry tools. These skills pay off next week when you start filtering traffic in Wireshark.

mentorQuote.title: Senior Security Engineer (Blue Team Lead), NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: CIDR isn’t just math — it’s how you define your attack surface. When I’m hunting threats in a network, the first thing I need to know is what address space I’m protecting. Every capture filter, every firewall rule, every ACL — they all start with understanding addresses. Get this right, and Wireshark becomes a precision instrument.

focusAreas: Binary, decimal, and hexadecimal number system conversions
CIDR notation and the visual count-out method
Calculating network addresses, broadcast addresses, and usable host ranges
Subnetting — borrowing bits and enumerating all resulting subnets
Documenting host and Kali VM network configurations
Creating a network topology diagram for the Gulf Coast engagement
objectives: Convert between decimal, binary, and hexadecimal number systems
Use the visual “count out” method to analyze CIDR notation
Calculate network addresses, broadcast addresses, and usable host ranges
Subnet a network and enumerate all resulting subnets
Document your local and VM network configurations

callouts.title: Save For Lab 3.3
callouts.text: This is Week 1 of a 3-week module. Create a folder called Module3_Screenshots and save EVERY screenshot there. You’ll compile these into your Lab 3.3 Engagement Packet in Week 3.

clientContext.description: Continuing their engagement with NEXUS after the cryptographic assessment in Module 2. Jennifer Walsh (CISO) has requested a network traffic analysis of their Azure-hosted patient portal. This week is internal training; next week you’ll dive into Wireshark; Week 3 you’ll capture live Azure traffic.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: This is Week 1 of a 3-week module. Log as Internal Training: 2–3 hours — Network addressing fundamentals. Create a folder called Module3_Screenshots and save EVERY screenshot there. You'll compile these into your Lab 3.3 Engagement Packet.

content.whatYoureDoing: Building fluency converting between decimal, binary, and hexadecimal number systems — the foundation for understanding network addresses.
content.whyItMatters: IP addresses are stored and processed as binary. MAC addresses use hexadecimal. Subnet masks make sense only when you see them in binary. Without number system fluency, CIDR notation and Wireshark capture filters are just magic incantations — with it, they become precise tools.

content.title: The Conversion Cheat Sheet
content.subtitle: Memorize these 16 values (0–15) and you can convert anything:
content.note: To convert 168 to hex: 168 = 160 + 8 = A0 + 08 = A8. To convert 252: 252 = 240 + 12 = F0 + 0C = FC.

steps.text: MAC Address: Given 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E, what is 2B in decimal?

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture your completed conversions (can be handwritten on paper). Save as 01-number-conversions.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Calculator shortcuts: Using online converters without understanding the math — you need this skill for quizzes
Place value errors: Forgetting that binary positions are powers of 2 (128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1)
Skipping verification: Always convert back to check your work

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: A complete conversion reference with all problems worked out. You’ll use these exact values in the next checkpoint.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 9 explains IP addressing and the TCP/IP protocol stack. Understanding binary representation is fundamental to network addressing.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: Why do network addresses use binary and hexadecimal instead of just decimal? Review Chapter 9’s coverage of IP addressing and the TCP/IP protocol stack.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: You just read Chapter 9. Add it to your references: Conklin, W. A., & White, G. (2021). Principles of Computer Security (6th ed.). McGraw-Hill. Chapter 9.

content.whatYoureDoing: Analyzing CIDR notation to determine network size, usable hosts, and address boundaries without subnetting.
content.whyItMatters: CIDR notation is the universal language for describing network size. When you see 192.168.1.0/24, you need to instantly know how many hosts that includes, where it starts, and where it ends. This is essential for firewall rules, capture filters, and vulnerability scans.

content.title: The Visual Count-Out Method
content.text:
For 192.168.0.0/22: Network: 22 bits | Host: 10 bits
11111111.11111111.11111100.00000000
Subnet Mask: 255.255.252.0 (FF.FF.FC.00)

steps.text: First usable host: ?   Last usable host: ?
Then repeat for 10.0.0.0/8 — a much larger network.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture your flat network analysis calculations. Save as 02-flat-network-analysis.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Forgetting to subtract 2: Network and broadcast addresses aren’t usable for hosts
CIDR confusion: /24 = 256 addresses, /25 = 128, /26 = 64 (each bit doubles/halves)
Boundary errors: Broadcast is ALL host bits = 1, not the next network address

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Your 192.168.0.0/22 analysis should show: 10 host bits, 1,022 usable hosts, broadcast 192.168.3.255. Your 10.0.0.0/8 should show: 24 host bits, 16,777,214 usable hosts.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 9 explains network addressing and CIDR notation. Understanding these calculations is fundamental to network segmentation.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: How does subnetting divide a network into smaller segments? Calculate the number of hosts in a /24 versus a /28 network.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: RFC 4632 — Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR): https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4632

content.whatYoureDoing: Creating a complete subnet table showing every possible subnet when you divide a larger network into smaller ones.
content.whyItMatters: Network segmentation is a core security control. By dividing networks into subnets, you can isolate sensitive systems, limit blast radius during incidents, and apply different security policies to different zones. This is why firewalls, VLANs, and access controls all depend on understanding subnet boundaries.

content.title: Borrowing Bits
content.text:
Starting with 192.168.0.0/22, borrow 2 host bits for subnets:
Network: 22 | Subnet: +2 | Host: 8
11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000
New CIDR: /24 — 4 subnets, 254 hosts each

steps.text: Starting with 172.16.0.0/16, borrow 4 bits for subnetting:
What is the new CIDR prefix? (16 + 4 = ?)
How many subnets can be created?
Create a table showing the first 4 subnets.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture your 172.16.0.0/16 subnet analysis with the table. Save as 03-subnet-table.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Forgetting the formula: 2n subnets when borrowing n bits
Host count confusion: Usable hosts = 2h − 2 (subtract network and broadcast)
Subnet boundary errors: Each subnet starts where the previous one ends — watch the increment pattern

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 9 discusses segregation, segmentation, and isolation. Subnetting creates security boundaries — the same technique you use here enables network architects to separate sensitive systems.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: Notice we’ve been using 10.x.x.x, 172.16.x.x, and 192.168.x.x — why these specific ranges? Look up RFC 1918 to understand private address space.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: RFC 1918 defines private addresses: Rekhter, Y., et al. (1996). RFC 1918: Address Allocation for Private Internets. IETF. https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1918

content.whatYoureDoing: Verifying that your hand calculations are correct using an industry-standard tool.
content.whyItMatters: In production environments, network configuration errors can cause outages or security gaps. Professionals always verify their calculations with trusted tools before implementing changes.

steps.text: Enter 192.168.0.0/22 and verify your Checkpoint 2 answers.
Enter 172.16.0.0/20 and verify your Checkpoint 3 answers.
If any calculations were wrong, go back and identify where you made the error.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture MX Toolbox results for one of your CIDR blocks. Save as 04-mxtoolbox-verification.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Skipping verification: Don’t assume your math is right — always check
Not correcting errors: If the tool shows different results, trace back to find your mistake

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: MX Toolbox results confirming your hand calculations for both /22 and /20 networks. If they don’t match, you’ve identified and corrected your errors.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 9 covers network troubleshooting and verification. Professional network engineers validate their work — MX Toolbox represents the external verification used in production environments.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: MX Toolbox offers more than just subnet calculations — explore their other network diagnostic tools. What else can you check about a domain or IP address?

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: MX Toolbox. (n.d.). Subnet Calculator. Retrieved from https://mxtoolbox.com/subnetcalculator.aspx

content.whatYoureDoing: Documenting your actual network configuration — both your host machine and your Kali VM.
content.whyItMatters: Every security engagement starts with understanding your own network position. Before you can analyze traffic or identify anomalies, you need to know what “normal” looks like for your environment. This baseline documentation is exactly what you’d create at the start of any client engagement or incident response.

steps.text: Document: IP Address, Subnet Mask (convert to CIDR), Default Gateway, DNS Server(s), Connection Type (WiFi or Ethernet).
Show ARP cache (IP to MAC mappings):
Document: Interface name, IP Address, Subnet Mask/CIDR, Default Gateway, ARP Cache entries, VirtualBox Network Mode (NAT, Bridged, etc.).

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture both your host network config and Kali’s ip addr output (VM name must be visible). Save as 05-host-kali-config.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Wrong adapter: Look for your active network adapter, not VirtualBox’s virtual adapters
VPN active: If using a VPN, your IP might show the VPN’s address
VM not running: Make sure Kali is booted and has network connectivity

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Complete network documentation for both your host machine and Kali VM: IP addresses, subnet masks in CIDR notation, default gateways, DNS servers, and VirtualBox network mode.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 9 emphasizes the importance of network documentation. Security teams can’t protect what they don’t know exists — your documentation creates the asset inventory that informs security controls.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: The ip command replaced the older ifconfig command. Run man ip to explore what else it can show you about your network configuration.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: You used Linux networking commands: ip(8) — Linux manual page. Retrieved from man ip

content.whatYoureDoing: Creating a visual diagram showing the network path from your Kali VM all the way to Gulf Coast’s Azure infrastructure.
content.whyItMatters: Network topology diagrams are essential for security assessments and incident response. They help teams understand traffic flows, identify chokepoints, and plan defensive controls. The diagram you create here is exactly what you’d include in a professional security report.

steps.text: Kali VM (with your IP from Checkpoint 5)
Host Machine (with your IP from Checkpoint 5)

content.title: Recommended Tools
content.text: app.diagrams.net (draw.io) is free and has network diagram shapes. PowerPoint, Visio, or even hand-drawn works too — we care about the content, not the polish.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Export your completed topology diagram. Save as 06-topology-diagram.png — this is your last screenshot for Lab 3.1!

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Missing labels: Every device and connection needs an IP address or network range labeled
Incomplete path: Show the entire chain from Kali VM through NAT, host, router, internet, to target
Low resolution export: Export as PNG at high resolution — blurry diagrams are unprofessional

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: A diagram showing the complete network path with IP addresses labeled at each hop. This diagram will appear in your Lab 3.3 Engagement Packet.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 9 discusses network topology and architecture visualization. Topology diagrams communicate network structure to stakeholders — a skill you’ll use in Lab 3.3’s client deliverable.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What is the difference between IPv4 and IPv6 addressing? Research the security improvements built into IPv6 (IPsec) and current adoption challenges.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: RFC 8200 — Internet Protocol Version 6. NIST SP 800-119 provides IPv6 deployment guidelines: https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-119/final

redTeam.label: Red Team Perspective
items: During reconnaissance, attackers identify network boundaries to understand scope
CIDR notation tells them how many hosts to scan, where subnets begin and end, and which ranges to target
Misconfigured subnet masks can expose more hosts than intended

blueTeam.label: Blue Team Perspective
items: Defenders use CIDR to define firewall rules, segment sensitive systems, and scope vulnerability scans
Proper subnetting creates security boundaries — an attacker who compromises one subnet shouldn’t automatically access others
Understanding addressing lets you spot anomalous traffic crossing boundaries

purpleTeam.connection: CIDR notation is the shared language between attackers and defenders. A red-team analyst uses subnet boundaries to scope a scan — knowing exactly which hosts to target and which to exclude. A blue-team analyst uses the same boundaries to write firewall rules, segment sensitive systems, and detect traffic crossing zone boundaries. When you calculate a subnet by hand, you're internalizing the same mental model both sides use.

screenshotTable.description: Complete subnet table for 172.16.0.0/16 with 4 bits borrowed

Host and Kali VM network configurations (VM name visible)

items: Number conversions (decimal, binary, hex)

Network topology diagram completed

finalChecklist.canvasQuiz: Take the Lab 3.1 Quiz in Canvas. Keep your notes handy — you may be asked to perform CIDR calculations or reference your network configuration.

needHelp.nextModule: Lab 3.2: Protocol Analysis Tools — Next week you’ll install Wireshark and dive into packet capture using training PCAPs. The network fundamentals you learned today — CIDR, subnets, address boundaries — make capture filters and display filters precise tools instead of guesswork.
needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late work is not accepted. Drop policies exist for emergencies.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Complete hands-on exercises on your behalf
genAI.principle: AI is a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

whatsNext.text: Lab 3.2: Protocol Analysis Tools — Next week you'll install Wireshark and dive deep into packet capture analysis using training PCAPs. You'll learn capture filters (using the CIDR skills you practiced today!), display filters, and traffic visualization. The network fundamentals from this lab make all of that possible.
